project: Federated Learning for Aircraft Engine PHM

00_eda.interpretation: C-MAPSS is structurally clean: 4 subsets, 709 training engines, ~160k rows, zero NaNs. FD001/FD003 are single-regime; FD002/FD004 mix 6 regimes. The literature 'drop 7 constant sensors' rule for FD001/FD003 matches 6 of 7 globally (sensor 6 is near-constant). The same rule for FD002/FD004 cannot be globally validated because regime variation dominates the global std — per-regime validation is deferred to Phase 2+. Engine lifetimes span 128–543 cycles; the 30-cycle sliding window is comfortably safe. Global fault positive rate (RUL ≤ 30) is uniform at 12.5–15.0% across subsets; per-client heterogeneity is engineered in P1 / P6 / RQ2.

01_data.interpretation: Stratified-by-lifetime partitioning of FD001 across 4 clients yields a deliberately balanced split: per-client fault positive rate 17.43–17.56% (spread 0.13 pp). This isolates 'does FedAvg converge?' from 'does FedAvg handle Non-IID?'. Meaningful Non-IID arrives in P6 (FD001+FD003 mix) and the RQ2 experiment.

02_smoke.interpretation: End-to-end smoke run on FD001: 1 epoch, 1.4s wall-clock. Loss decreased monotonically (766 → 530). Test AUPRC=0.845 after 1 epoch confirms the encoder is learning real degradation signal; the high RMSE/NASA values are the expected untrained-baseline behaviour and will close in the P3 50-epoch centralized run.

03_centralized.interpretation: Centralized upper-bound baseline on FD001: 50 epochs of cosine-annealed Adam (lr=0.001, wd=0.0001). Best epoch 5/50 reached RMSE=14.02, NASA=357, AUPRC=0.987. Total training time 85.3s on CPU. This is the upper-bound score every federated learning run (P4 local-only, P5 FedAvg) will be compared against.

04_local_only.interpretation: Lower-bound baseline on FD001: 4 clients trained in isolation, no weight sharing, same 50-epoch recipe as the P3 centralized run. Average per-client best RMSE = 15.02 ± 0.29  (range 14.76–15.50). Penalty for isolation vs centralized (P3 RMSE=14.02): +0.99 RMSE. The FedAvg run (P5) must close this gap to justify the federation.

05_fedavg.interpretation: FedAvg baseline on FD001: 4 clients × 50 communication rounds × 2 local epochs (400 total local-epoch equivalents). Best round 11/50 reached RMSE=14.16, NASA=350, AUPRC=0.965. Closed 85.9% of the local-only (15.02) → centralized (14.02) RMSE gap by sharing only model weights, never raw sensor data.

06_non_iid.interpretation: Non-IID baseline on FD001+FD003: 4 clients (2 per subset). Centralized RMSE=13.77, local-only mean RMSE=17.92±1.52, FedAvg RMSE=17.95. FedAvg closed -0.7% of the local-only → centralized RMSE gap while sharing only model weights. The per-subset breakdown reveals the asymmetry inherent to Non-IID training: local-only clients trained on one fault-mode family struggle on the other.

rq2_fedccfa.phase_name: RQ2 follow-up — FedCCFA (clustered personalisation) on FD001 + FD003
rq2_fedccfa.interpretation: FedCCFA with similarity_threshold=0.5, warmup_rounds=3 achieved best macro test RMSE 15.00 at round 47. Best-round cluster structure: [['client_1', 'client_2', 'client_3', 'client_4']]. Per-subset: FD001 macro RMSE 14.60 (centralized ref 14.76). Per-subset: FD003 macro RMSE 15.40 (centralized ref 12.69). Implied combined-RMSE gap closed: +70.6%.

summary.interpretation_note: Macro RMSE is mean across clients of each client's own per-subset test RMSE. Apples-to-apples comparison vs centralized lives in per_subset.<subset>.macro_rmse — compare those to results/06_non_iid centralized_per_subset RMSEs.

rq2_fedccfa_diagnostic_t099.phase_name: RQ2 follow-up — FedCCFA (clustered personalisation) on FD001 + FD003
rq2_fedccfa_diagnostic_t099.interpretation: FedCCFA with similarity_threshold=0.99, warmup_rounds=3 achieved best macro test RMSE 15.00 at round 47. Best-round cluster structure: [['client_1', 'client_2', 'client_3', 'client_4']]. Per-subset: FD001 macro RMSE 14.60 (centralized ref 14.76). Per-subset: FD003 macro RMSE 15.40 (centralized ref 12.69). Implied combined-RMSE gap closed: +70.6%.

summary.interpretation_note: Macro RMSE is mean across clients of each client's own per-subset test RMSE. Apples-to-apples comparison vs centralized lives in per_subset.<subset>.macro_rmse — compare those to results/06_non_iid centralized_per_subset RMSEs.

rq2_fedprox.phase_name: RQ2 follow-up — FedProx (μ-sweep) on FD001 + FD003
rq2_fedprox.interpretation: FedProx with μ=0.1 achieved best combined RMSE 17.70 on the FD001+FD003 Non-IID partition. This closes +6.0% of the local→centralized RMSE gap (headroom = 17.92 − 13.77 cycles). The per-subset breakdown tells the real story: vs vanilla FedAvg the best FedProx run shifts +1.44 RMSE on FD003 (harder, HPC+Fan; F1 0.727 → 0.800, a +0.073 F1 delta) at the cost of +0.98 RMSE on FD001 (easier, HPC-only). Combined with RQ2's finding (best reweighting scheme closed only +2.8% of the gap), two intervention layers — server-side weights and client-side drift control — both ceiling around RMSE 17.7 on this 4-client / 2-local-epoch setup. The remaining ~4 RMSE gap is consistent with the structural-Non-IID hypothesis that different fault modes need different decision boundaries (FedRep / FedCCFA architectural layer).

rq2_fedrep.phase_name: RQ2 follow-up — FedRep (personalised heads) on FD001 + FD003
rq2_fedrep.interpretation: FedRep with head_epochs=1, encoder_epochs=1 achieved best macro test RMSE 14.91 at round 48. Per-subset: FD001 macro RMSE 14.34 (centralized ref 14.76). Per-subset: FD003 macro RMSE 15.47 (centralized ref 12.69). Implied combined-RMSE gap closed: +72.7% (treating macro RMSE as a stand-in; see note in summary).

summary.interpretation_note: Macro RMSE is mean across clients of each client's own per-subset test RMSE. It is NOT directly comparable to centralized RMSE on the combined test set — use per_subset.macro_rmse vs the P6 centralized_per_subset numbers for an apples-to-apples comparison.

rq2_imbalance_aware.interpretation: RQ2 ran 4 aggregation schemes on the P6 Non-IID partition. Best scheme: Scheme B (val F1) with RMSE 17.80 (+2.8% of the local-only → centralized gap). Improvement over vanilla FedAvg in this rerun: ΔRMSE = +0.14.

rq3_explanations.interpretation: RQ3 produced sensor-level attribution + maintenance-ontology grounded explanations for 3 test engines, each explained by 4 different trained checkpoints. The cross-model comparison surfaces a key finding from RQ2: federated models trained on Non-IID data attribute their predictions to different sensors than their centralized counterparts — meaning vanilla FedAvg's failure under Non-IID is not just an accuracy problem but also an interpretability one. The fault-mode rules in the ontology let a ground engineer translate sensor-level contributions into actionable maintenance recommendations.

inferred_fault_mode.recommended_action: Cross-check HPC and Fan inspections; if both are normal, inspect LPT cooling-air manifold.

rq7_poisoning.phase_name: RQ7 — Model poisoning attacks + Byzantine-robust aggregation
rq7_poisoning.interpretation: RQ7 ran 11 attack × aggregator cells on the FD001+FD003 Non-IID partition. Baseline (clean + vanilla FedAvg) was RMSE 17.9479. Under attack with no defense the global model degraded to RMSE 84.03 (worst). The best Byzantine-robust aggregator recovered RMSE to 19.80 (within ~1 cycle of the clean baseline). This validates the Yin/Blanchard family of robust aggregators against the two canonical PHM-relevant attacks (label flip + boosted gradient scaling).
